reasoning:
The exploit's core claim — that `totalBytes` accounting in `safeExtract` happens post-entry rather than per-chunk, creating a window where multiple entries' data is in memory before the cap is enforced — is structurally correct but overstated in severity.

Actual code path in extract.ts:
- `writeFileEntry` (line 241-280) registers `entry.on('data', chunk => chunks.push(chunk))` and resolves only after `entry.on('end')`. `hooks.addTotal(bytes)` is called at line 273, AFTER the end event resolves the inner Promise.
- The tar parser (tar@7.5.20, read-entry.js line 38) starts every ReadEntry paused. When `writeFileEntry` calls `entry.on('data', ...)` (Minipass `on()` at minipass line 638-643), Minipass auto-resumes the stream. But `writeFileEntry` first awaits `fs.mkdir()` (line 251) before registering the data listener, so the entry stays paused until that async I/O completes.
- While Entry N is waiting on its `mkdir`, tar's CONSUMECHUNKSUB loop continues synchronously writing Entry N+1's body chunks into that entry's Minipass internal buffer (via `entry.write()` → `BUFFERPUSH`). This means Entry N's `chunks[]` (up to maxEntryBytes ≈ 50MB) and Entry N+1's Minipass internal buffer (up to 50MB) co-exist in memory before Entry N's `hooks.addTotal` has run.
- The PROCESSENTRY call for Entry N+1 fires synchronously inside Entry N's `end` handler chain (parse.js line 281: `entry.on('end', () => this[NEXTENTRY]())`), so Entry N+1's data can begin accumulating before the microtask queue runs Entry N's `hooks.addTotal`.

Why the exploit overstates the effect: `createReadStream` reads in 64KB chunks. The pipeline respects backpressure — when Minipass write() returns false (not-flowing), the parser signals not-ready, stalling the read stream. This limits how much data accumulates across entries before I/O yields. In practice, ~2-3 entries' worth of data can overlap, not 20. The "20 concurrent entries → 1 GB" scenario requires feeding all 1GB of compressed data in a single synchronous write to the parser, which the 64KB-chunk pipeline prevents.

What IS real: any single `writeFileEntry` call holds a full entry's decompressed chunks (up to 50MB) in `chunks[]` PLUS `Buffer.concat(chunks)` simultaneously at line 274 (2x per-entry peak). The totalBytes accounting fires after the entry finishes but BEFORE `fs.writeFile` completes — so two entries can overlap: one in `chunks[]` + concat buffer, one in Minipass internal buffer. That is ~150-200MB overhead above the intended cap, not 1GB.

The accounting-after-entry pattern (rather than per-chunk) is a genuine design flaw. The fix described (move accounting into the `data` handler) would close this gap. But the claim of "1GB RSS from 20 concurrent entries" requires conditions the Node.js event loop does not produce with a file-backed pipeline.
refutation: The exploit correctly identifies a real accounting race: `hooks.addTotal` fires at line 273 of extract.ts only after the entry's `end` event, not per-chunk. However, the claimed impact of 20 entries accumulating 1GB simultaneously is refuted by Node.js stream backpressure. `createReadStream` feeds data in 64KB chunks; Minipass/tar emit backpressure signals when entries are paused, stalling the pipeline. The realistic worst case is 2-3 entries overlapping (~150-200MB above cap), not 20 (1GB). The OOM-of-a-7GB-runner claim is implausible under normal I/O conditions.
concrete_test: Build a 20-entry tarball (each entry 50MB-1 byte). Run safeExtract on it and measure Node.js RSS at peak. If the exploit's claim held, RSS would reach ~1GB before any totalBytes error. With backpressure in place, RSS peaks at ~150-200MB (2-3 entries overlapping), not 1GB. An additional test: move `hooks.addTotal(bytes)` into the `data` handler (before `chunks.push`) and rerun — RSS should stay bounded to maxEntryBytes + overhead.

A pre-planted file at `~/.cache/vetlock/analysis/<mangled-integrity>.json` with `{"name":"lodash","version":"4.17.21","formatVersion":1,"files":[],"nativeArtifacts":[],...}` will pass the cache-hit guard. All detectors then run on an empty snapshot and produce no findings. GHSA correlation in detectors/index.ts lines 139-149 iterates over findings — with zero findings the loop body never executes, so no advisory annotation fires. The verdict remains CLEAN, exit 0. The attack works for any package that appears as `added` or `upgraded` in the changeset (new version or new package in the diff). The `integrity-changed` BLOCK path in engine.ts lines 148-169 does NOT interfere here because that path fires only when the same (name, version) exists in BOTH old and new graphs with differing integrity strings; an attacker introducing a new version or a brand-new package bypasses that check entirely. The exploit's primary reproduction scenario (lodash@4.17.20 unchanged between before/after) is not actually exploitable — computeChangeset emits no change entry for it, so analyzeOne is never called and the cache is never consulted. However the "compositional twist" case (new or upgraded package) is the real attack vector and does work end-to-end as described. The verdict is 'plausible' rather than 'confirmed' because end-to-end verification requires filesystem write access to the cache directory on the CI runner, which is the stated precondition; the code path itself is fully traceable but the exploit was not run live.

If lodash@4.17.20 appears identically in both before and after, no change entry is produced, analyzeOne is never called, and the cache is never queried — the planted poison file is completely irrelevant. The claim that the main scenario produces CLEAN because the cache serves benign data is wrong for the "unchanged integrity" sub-case; it produces CLEAN for a trivially benign reason (no diff), not because of cache poisoning. The "compositional twist" (attacker changes the integrity) is the real attack, but the integrity-changed BLOCK path would fire if the same version has different integrity — the attacker must therefore use a NEW version or NEW package name, not just swap the integrity hash of an existing version. The exploit's description conflates two different sub-cases.

reasoning:
The exploit claims that a YAML anchor/alias bomb in a pnpm-lock.yaml causes `yaml.load()` to allocate ~46MB (or more) of heap and crash vetlock with "Invalid string length" or OOM, because `parsePnpmLockText` (lockfile-pnpm.ts:53) calls `yaml.load(yamlText)` with no alias limits.

The exploit's measurement — `JSON.stringify(r).length === 46913603` — is correct, but the heap allocation is not. js-yaml 4.3.0 resolves YAML aliases by reference assignment (`state.result = state.anchorMap[alias]` in loader.js:1382), not by deep-copying the referenced structure. When parsing `b: &b [*a,*a,...,*a]`, each `*a` element in the resulting array is a pointer to the same `a` array object, not a clone. The actual heap allocated by `yaml.load()` for the 7-level bomb fixture is ~57KB (verified empirically: `heap delta = 57024 bytes`), not 46MB.

Furthermore, `parsePnpmLockText` only accesses `doc.packages`, `doc.snapshots`, `doc.importers`, `doc.lockfileVersion`, and the top-level dependency maps. It never accesses `doc._bomb` or calls any function that traverses the bomb structure recursively (no `JSON.stringify`, no deep clone). The `_bomb` key is silently ignored.

Empirical verification: running the exact fixture through `parsePnpmLockText` returns `SUCCESS: nodes: 2, byName keys: ['malicious']` — the parser succeeds normally, the malicious package is parsed and would proceed to full analysis. No crash, no DoS, no bypass.

The 46MB figure is `JSON.stringify` output length — a serialization artifact irrelevant to vetlock since `parsePnpmLockText` never serializes `doc`. The `_bomb` key is never accessed: the function only reads `doc.packages`, `doc.snapshots`, `doc.importers`, and top-level dep maps. `parsePnpmLockText` returns successfully with the malicious package correctly parsed and queued for analysis. Confirmed by running the fixture directly: `SUCCESS: nodes: 2, byName keys: ['malicious']`.

reasoning:
The exploit claims that a pnpm lockfile whose `peerDependencies` are omitted from the dep-merge dict in `lockfile-pnpm.ts:138-143` allows a malicious peer-only node to pass vetlock with empty `provenance: []`. The code defect in the pnpm parser is real: `lockfile-pnpm.ts:138-143` only merges `dependencies` and `optionalDependencies` (from both `inline` and `snap`), never `peerDependencies`. The npm parser at `lockfile.ts:123-127` does include `...val.peerDependencies` in its merge. The yarn parser at `lockfile-yarn.ts:143-146` also omits peerDependencies.

However, the exploit cannot produce CLEAN/WARN output because the CLI does not feed pnpm graphs to the engine. `cli.ts:177-191` shows a two-step design: `parseLockfileText` is called as a preflight check only (comment at line 178-180 explicitly states "runDiff still assumes npm"). The actual `runDiff(beforeText, afterText, ...)` call at line 191 passes the raw text strings to `engine.ts:runDiff`, which at lines 77-78 calls `parseLockfile(JSON.parse(oldLockfileText))` — the npm JSON parser. A pnpm-lock.yaml file is YAML, not JSON; `JSON.parse` throws a SyntaxError, which propagates through the catch at cli.ts:202-204 and causes `process.exit(3)`. The attacker's pnpm lockfile never reaches the graph traversal, the changeset computation, or any detector. The verdict output is a crash, not CLEAN.

The exploit's stated reproduction path (run `vetlock diff lockfile.before.yaml lockfile.after.yaml`) would exit with code 3 and a parse error, not produce a behavioral verdict. The code defect (missing peerDep merge) is a latent bug that matters only once the pnpm-to-engine integration is completed (the TODO at cli.ts:179), but in the current codebase it is not reachable via the described attack path.
refutation: The CLI calls `runDiff(beforeText, afterText)` which internally does `JSON.parse(oldLockfileText)` at engine.ts:77. pnpm-lock.yaml is YAML, not JSON. JSON.parse throws a SyntaxError on YAML input before any graph is built, any changeset is computed, or any detector is called. The exploit scenario requires pnpm YAML input to be analyzed, but that input crashes the process before reaching the analysis layer. Code path: cli.ts:191 → engine.ts:runDiff:77 → `parseLockfile(JSON.parse(yamlText))` → SyntaxError → caught at cli.ts:202 → `process.exit(3)`. No CLEAN or WARN verdict is possible.
concrete_test: Supply a pnpm-lock.yaml (containing lockfileVersion: '9.0' and a peer-only payload node) as both before and after arguments to `vetlock diff`. The process exits with code 3 and prints a JSON parse error to stderr. No findings array, no verdict field, no CLEAN output. This can be verified by running: `echo "lockfileVersion: '9.0'" > /tmp/test.yaml && node packages/cli/dist/cli.js diff /tmp/test.yaml /tmp/test.yaml --json 2>&1; echo "exit: $?"` — expected: a JSON SyntaxError on stderr and exit code 3.
